name: Teacher Appraisal Digest
one_liner: Pulls performance reviews, observation notes, and 360 feedback from TechOne HRIS and Google Drive, then generates a 2-page NESA/CHED-aligned appraisal summary for each educator — cutting a 40-hour cycle down to minutes.
problem: Principals and HR managers overseeing 100–500 educators collect appraisal data across email, Google Docs, and TechOne HRIS, then spend 40+ hours per cycle reading unstructured feedback and manually mapping it to NESA Professional Teaching Standards or CHED faculty competency frameworks. The synthesis is subjective, slow, and produces inconsistent quality across campuses.
ai_technique: Summarization & Condensation with domain-specific prompt engineering
how_it_works: Connects to TechOne HRIS, Outlook, and Google Drive. Extracts observation notes, student and peer feedback, and prior review documents per educator. Runs a multi-step pipeline: classify feedback against NESA or CHED competency domains, extract evidence quotes, then generate a structured 2-page digest covering performance against each standard, gaps, and promotion readiness. Output is a principal-ready PDF or Word document, not a raw data dump.
buyer_persona: Principal or Head of HR managing 50-500 educators across distributed campuses
scoring_rationale: Strong pain point with a clear buyer (HR director, principal) and a concrete deliverable achievable in one term. Scores moderately on defensibility — the NESA/CHED domain mapping creates some stickiness, but the core technique is summarization any GPT wrapper could replicate without the integration layer.

name: Staff Data Integration Hub
one_liner: Reconciles payroll, HRIS, state teaching registration, and PD tracking into a single staff record — so HR can answer 'who needs PD renewal?' without opening four systems.
problem: Education HR teams at CSPD and PHINMA cross-reference TechOne payroll, state teaching registration databases (NESA, QTSB, CHED), appraisal spreadsheets, and a separate PD tracker to answer routine compliance questions. Each data reconciliation is manual, error-prone, and blocks workforce analytics. A single expired registration discovered late creates legal exposure and operational disruption.
ai_technique: Data Transformation & Structuring with education schema normalization
how_it_works: Ingests scheduled exports from TechOne (payroll/HRIS), calls state registration APIs (NESA, QTSB, CHED), and reads local PD tracking sheets via Google Sheets or CSV. Normalizes employment type, teaching area, registration status, PD hours, and contract end dates into a canonical staff schema. Flags mismatches — expired registration, PD hours below required threshold, contract end approaching — and exposes a queryable API and dashboard for downstream HR tools.
buyer_persona: Chief People Officer or IT Manager responsible for HR data integrity
scoring_rationale: Real pain but this is fundamentally a data integration project — ETL and schema normalization — with limited agentic value. Buyer clarity is mixed: the CPO wants the outcome but IT owns the implementation, creating a sales complication. Build time is underestimated given state API variability across jurisdictions.

name: Teacher Attrition Risk Forecast
one_liner: Ranks every teacher in the network by 6–12 month resignation probability, using salary position, contract length, location hardship, and subject scarcity as predictors — so HR can run stay interviews before the resignation letter arrives.
problem: Rising Academies and PHINMA cannot predict teacher churn until it happens. Each unexpected departure in a scarce subject (STEM, special education) triggers a 4–6 month emergency recruitment cycle and costs 6–12 months of salary in replacement overheads. HR teams have the data — tenure, salary relative to market, remote posting, certification expiry — but no model connecting it to attrition outcomes.
ai_technique: Time Series Forecasting with education-specific feature engineering
how_it_works: Trains on historical HRIS attrition records (voluntary departures by role, campus, tenure band) with features including salary percentile versus local market, contract type, days to registration expiry, enrollment stability at assigned school, consecutive remote postings, and subject scarcity index. Outputs a ranked risk queue — updated monthly — showing each educator's risk band (low/medium/high) with the top contributing factors. HR uses the high-risk list to schedule stay conversations; the system tracks whether interventions changed outcomes.
buyer_persona: Head of HR or Talent Operations managing hiring pipelines for 200+ educators
scoring_rationale: High severity — unexpected churn in remote or specialist roles is an acute operational crisis with quantifiable cost. Defensibility improves over time as the model trains on client-specific attrition patterns. Main risk: needs 2–3 years of historical HRIS data to train reliably, which early-stage clients in West Africa may not have in structured form.

name: Professional Development Scheduler
one_liner: Schedules mandatory PD across 80+ campuses by resolving conflicts between certification deadlines, award-condition day limits, and campus operational calendars — automatically.
problem: Networks running 500+ educators across distributed campuses (Rising Academies, School for Life) must schedule mandatory PD — child safety, curriculum renewal, accreditation requirements — without breaching award conditions that cap PD days per year. Manual scheduling by an administrator produces conflicts, last-minute cancellations, and compliance gaps that surface during regulatory audits.
ai_technique: Resource Allocation & Scheduling with constraint satisfaction
how_it_works: Ingests educator certification status and expiry dates from HRIS, award-condition constraints (maximum PD days, release-time entitlements by employment type), and campus operational calendars (exam blocks, public holidays, enrolment periods). Runs a constraint-satisfaction solver to assign each mandatory PD block to the earliest available slot that respects all constraints. Pushes confirmed schedules to Outlook/Google Calendar and triggers reminder notifications 30 and 7 days before each session.
buyer_persona: Professional Development Coordinator or HR Manager at multi-site operator
scoring_rationale: Scheduling pain is real but not severe — most networks tolerate some manual coordination. Low defensibility: constraint-based scheduling is a solved problem (Google Calendar, Deputy, Humanforce already do parts of this). Weak standalone business model; most valuable as a feature inside a broader HR platform.

name: Enrollment Intent Classifier
one_liner: Reads inbound SMS, WhatsApp, and email inquiries from prospective families and classifies them by enrollment intent and dropout risk — so admissions staff call the right people first.
problem: Admissions teams at Rising Academies and iPeople receive hundreds of weekly inquiries across SMS, WhatsApp, and email in markets where conversion rates sit at 5–15%. Staff manually read every message to decide who to call — missing cost-sensitive families who need a scholarship nudge and flagging high-intent families too late. Early dropout signals ('can't afford fees', 'might relocate') embedded in inquiry text go undetected until the student leaves.
ai_technique: Text Classification with education-specific intent taxonomy
how_it_works: Connects to WhatsApp Business API, email inbox, and web form submissions. Classifies each message into: high-intent enrollment, scholarship inquiry, fee-payment concern, program comparison, or dropout-risk. For dropout-risk flags, extracts the underlying signal (financial, geographic, academic) and routes to a student success officer with a suggested response. High-intent leads go to an admissions rep within the hour. Weekly digest shows funnel conversion by intent category, helping teams measure which outreach scripts work.
buyer_persona: Enrollment Manager or Student Success Officer at multi-campus or international institution
scoring_rationale: High immediate ROI — even a 2-percentage-point lift in conversion in a 10,000-inquiry market covers the product cost many times over. Time to value is fast: the classifier can be trained on historical WhatsApp exports and deployed in weeks. Duplicate risk is significant: ideas 17, 39, 72 in the full list all describe the same core concept.

name: Cohort Retention Predictor
one_liner: Forecasts semester-by-semester completion probability for each enrolled student in a multi-year degree program, so academic support teams intervene before the dropout decision is made.
problem: PHINMA and iPeople lose 20–30% of students between semester 1 and semester 4 — after the institution has already invested in onboarding and the student has invested in fees. Finance teams cannot model revenue reliably because dropout timing is unpredictable. Academic support resources (tutoring, scholarships) are allocated reactively rather than to the students most likely to leave.
ai_technique: Time Series Forecasting with academic progression patterns
how_it_works: Trains on cohort enrollment history linked to semester GPA, fee payment timeliness (early indicator of financial stress), first-generation student status, and attendance proxies. Outputs per-student retention probability updated each semester, with a ranked at-risk queue for the academic support team. Integrates with the student information system to pull new data each semester without manual exports. Tracks whether interventions (tutoring referral, emergency scholarship) reduced dropout.
buyer_persona: Registrar or Student Success Officer in multi-year degree programs
scoring_rationale: Genuine problem with quantifiable revenue impact, but buyer clarity is blurred between Registrar (operational), VP Academic (strategic), and CFO (financial). Needs 3–4 years of historical cohort data to train — a constraint for newer institutions. Academic early-warning systems (EAB Navigate, Civitas Learning) exist in the US market; differentiation depends on localization for African/SEA contexts.

name: Student Enrollment Propensity Scorer
one_liner: Scores each applicant's probability of enrolling and completing semester 2 using academic record, family background, and geographic signals — so recruitment spend targets families most likely to become paying students.
problem: PHINMA processes 20,000+ applications annually but cannot distinguish applicants who will enroll and stay from those price-shopping across five institutions. Marketing budgets are spread across the full applicant pool rather than concentrated on high-propensity candidates. Recruiters spend equal time on applicants with near-zero conversion probability as on those who need a single follow-up call to commit.
ai_technique: Structured Data Classification with enrollment decision modeling
how_it_works: Trains on historical application records linked to enrollment and retention outcomes. Features include academic qualification tier, prior school type (private/public), distance from nearest campus, inquiry source (WhatsApp referral vs. paid ad), and family background proxies where available. Scores each new applicant 0–100 for 'probability of enrolling and reaching semester 2'. Generates three priority tiers for the admissions team: high-propensity (personal follow-up), medium (scholarship-focused outreach), low (automated response only). Refreshes scores weekly as new inquiry signals arrive.
buyer_persona: Director of Admissions or Marketing Manager at HE institution
scoring_rationale: Clear ROI story — shifting recruitment effort from the full applicant pool to top-scoring prospects directly reduces cost-per-enrolment. Buyer (Director of Admissions) has budget authority and feels this pain. Functional overlap with ideas 19 and 74 in the full list requires careful positioning; the differentiation here is the semester-2 retention signal baked into the score.

name: Tuition Price Elasticity Model
one_liner: Estimates how enrollment changes when tuition or scholarship offers change — giving CFOs a data-backed basis for pricing decisions instead of gut feel.
problem: PHINMA and iPeople set tuition levels and scholarship budgets annually based on last year's numbers and competitor word-of-mouth. They do not know whether a 5% fee increase will cost 200 enrollments or 20. Scholarship funds are allocated by custom rather than by modeled impact on conversion rates, leaving money on the table or over-investing in discounts that would have enrolled anyway.
ai_technique: Regression & Quantitative Estimation with pricing elasticity modeling
how_it_works: Analyzes 3–5 years of enrollment data by program, campus, and cohort, matched to the tuition and scholarship offer in effect at the time of each decision. Fits a demand elasticity model for each program cluster: '5% tuition increase correlates with 8% enrollment decline in nursing; 3% in engineering.' Separately models scholarship effectiveness: the marginal enrollment gain per peso/naira of scholarship spend, segmented by applicant income proxy. Outputs a pricing scenario tool — CFOs input a proposed tuition level and scholarship budget, and the model returns an enrollment and revenue forecast with confidence intervals.
buyer_persona: Chief Financial Officer or VP of Strategic Planning in HE institution
scoring_rationale: CFO is a clear budget holder but the problem is annual and strategic rather than daily and acute, reducing urgency. Time to value is long — the model needs historical data and a full pricing cycle to validate predictions. Niche enough to be defensible but small enough that few institutions would pay for it as a standalone product; stronger as a module inside a broader enrollment intelligence platform.

name: Cross-Campus Variance Scanner
one_liner: Compares teaching standard compliance, curriculum fidelity, and pastoral scores across every school in a network, surfacing outliers and peer-learning opportunities in a weekly digest.
problem: A network of 80 Catholic schools (CSPD) has no continuous visibility into quality variance across sites. Annual inspections identify problems months after they develop. Principals with high-performing programs have no mechanism to share practice with underperforming peers. Network leadership makes resource allocation decisions without knowing which schools are drifting on NESA standard compliance or pastoral care implementation.
ai_technique: Summarization & Condensation with structured comparison analysis
how_it_works: Ingests classroom observation reports (NESA-aligned scoring rubrics), student feedback surveys, and curriculum audit data from TechOne and Google Forms. For each school, calculates performance across standard domains and benchmarks against the network average. Generates a weekly variance report: schools above and below network average by domain, with the specific evidence driving the gap ('School A pastoral score 1.4 points below network; observation notes cite inconsistent behaviour management'). Flags schools improving fastest (candidate peer coaches) and those deteriorating (prioritize principal support visit).
buyer_persona: Network Director or Chief Academic Officer overseeing 40+ school sites
scoring_rationale: Network leaders at CSPD face exactly this problem and have no current tool for it — high greenfield advantage. The pain is real and daily: without variance data, support resources are allocated by relationship rather than evidence. Main build risk is data quality of observation reports, which are often unstructured narrative rather than scored rubrics.

name: Teacher Development Portfolio Engine
one_liner: Consolidates a teacher's PD records, observation scores, and 360 feedback into a structured career portfolio, then shows the specific gaps between their current profile and promotion criteria.
problem: At CSPD and PHINMA, a teacher's development record is scattered: PD completion in Moodle, observation scores in a Google Sheet, peer feedback in email. When a principal evaluates promotion, they reconstruct this narrative manually from 5–6 sources — and often miss gaps. Teachers themselves have no visibility into where they stand relative to senior teacher criteria, which frustrates retention and career motivation.
ai_technique: Data Transformation & Structuring with career-progression schema
how_it_works: Ingests PD records from Moodle and in-house LMS, observation reports from TechOne, and 360 feedback from email or form submissions. Normalizes into a structured portfolio: PD hours by domain (pedagogy, subject knowledge, digital literacy), NESA or CHED standard mastery band per domain, peer feedback themes, and student outcome metrics where available. Maps the teacher's profile against published senior teacher and master teacher advancement criteria. Generates a gap report: 'You need 18 more ICT-domain PD hours and one peer-mentoring role to meet senior teacher criteria — here are three upcoming courses that count.'
buyer_persona: Head of Teacher Development or Professional Learning Communities Manager
scoring_rationale: Useful but not urgent — teachers can survive without a portfolio view, so the pain is moderate. Buyer persona (Head of Teacher Development) often lacks direct budget authority. Build is feasible but data normalization across multiple LMS platforms is tedious and slows time to value. Strongest argument is the retention angle: visible career progression reduces early attrition.

name: Organizational Health Monitor
one_liner: Aggregates HRIS turnover data, pulse survey responses, and exit interview themes into a quarterly health scorecard per campus — so network leaders see deterioration before a crisis hits.
problem: Network leaders at Kinetic, CSPD, and PHINMA learn about campus-level culture problems after the fact: a cluster of resignations, a failing principal, a community complaint. Quarterly all-staff surveys arrive too late and with too much noise. There is no signal that aggregates the leading indicators — elevated turnover in one team, drop in engagement scores, concentration of exit interview themes — into a campus-level health picture.
ai_technique: Summarization & Condensation with health metric aggregation
how_it_works: Pulls monthly data from HRIS (voluntary turnover rate by role and campus), pulse survey tools (Officevibe, SurveyMonkey, email-based), appraisal ratings, and exit interview notes. Applies NLP to exit interview text to extract recurring themes (workload, leadership, pay, career). Generates a quarterly campus scorecard: engagement index, 90-day rolling attrition rate, capability maturity band, and the top three culture themes from qualitative sources. Flags campuses in red (two or more indicators deteriorating) and surfaces the evidence so the network director can prioritize support visits.
buyer_persona: Chief People Officer or Network Director managing 40+ distributed campuses
scoring_rationale: The concept is sound but relies heavily on organizations having structured pulse survey data — which many of the target clients do not run consistently. Low agentic suitability: this is primarily a reporting and aggregation problem, not a reasoning chain problem. Overlaps with existing HR analytics platforms; differentiation requires the education-specific framing to be meaningfully different from generic people analytics.

name: Regulatory Compliance Status Tracker
one_liner: Connects to NESA, QTSB, and CHED registration APIs and the in-house LMS to maintain a live compliance dashboard — flagging expired registrations and mandatory training gaps before they become liability events.
problem: Schools at CSPD and Rising Academies carry legal exposure every day a teacher works with an expired registration or overdue child safety clearance. HR teams track compliance in spreadsheets that fall out of sync as teachers complete training through different channels. A compliance gap discovered during an audit — or after an incident — creates serious regulatory and reputational consequences.
ai_technique: Data Transformation & Structuring with compliance schema and alert logic
how_it_works: Makes API calls to state registration authorities (NESA, QTSB, CHED) on a weekly schedule and compares returned status against the active staff list in TechOne HRIS. Pulls mandatory training completion records from the LMS (child safety, CPR, curriculum updates). Maintains a live compliance matrix per teacher: registration status, expiry date, training completion, and days to next renewal. Generates three outputs: a dashboard for HR (overall compliance %, names of non-compliant staff), automated email reminders to affected teachers at 60, 30, and 7 days before expiry, and an audit-ready compliance report in PDF.
buyer_persona: Compliance Officer or Head of HR responsible for regulatory adherence
scoring_rationale: High severity — an unregistered teacher on payroll is a liability event, not a minor inconvenience. Registration APIs exist and are callable. Time to value is fast: a working compliance dashboard can be deployed in under 8 weeks. The weak spot is defensibility — the core logic is rule-based alerting, not deep AI — but domain specificity (knowing which APIs to call and which frameworks to map to) creates real switching costs.

name: Teacher Resume Parser & Matcher
one_liner: Extracts degree, subject specialization, teaching registration, and experience from CV uploads, then auto-ranks candidates against open role specifications — so recruiters review matched shortlists, not raw piles.
problem: CSPD and PHINMA post 200+ educator positions annually. Recruiters open every CV to manually extract qualification details, verify subject matches, and check whether a NESA or CHED registration is current — a process that takes 20–30 minutes per application. Unqualified candidates consume the same review time as strong matches. Compliance risk occurs when registration status is not cross-checked and an unregistered teacher is advanced to interview.
ai_technique: Structured Data Classification with resume parsing
how_it_works: Accepts CV uploads via email attachment or a web portal. Runs a structured extraction pipeline: degree and major (B.Ed, B.Sc + teaching diploma), subject specialization, years of experience by grade band, state registration status claimed on CV, and additional certifications (IB, Cambridge, special education). Matches extracted profile against open role requirements stored in the system ('STEM teacher, secondary, NESA-registered, 3+ years'). Returns a ranked shortlist for each open role, with a match score and the specific evidence. Flags candidates claiming registration for a mandatory verification check against the NESA/QTSB API.
buyer_persona: Recruiting Manager or HR Specialist responsible for educator hiring
scoring_rationale: Recruiter time savings are immediate and measurable — a 50% reduction in CV review time per hire is easy to demonstrate. Education-specific extraction (teaching registration, IB certification, subject specialization) differentiates this from generic ATS resume parsing. Low defensibility risk: domain-specific ontology and API verification layer creates meaningful moat over a generic GPT wrapper.

name: Predictive Hiring Pipeline Forecast
one_liner: Forecasts teacher hiring demand by role and campus 6–12 months ahead, so HR teams source candidates before positions become urgent rather than after.
problem: Rising Academies and PHINMA discover most hiring needs 4–8 weeks before a term starts — too late for quality recruitment that takes 4–6 months for specialist roles. Enrollment spikes at one campus, planned school expansion, and predictable attrition are all knowable in advance, but no system connects these signals to a forward-looking staffing requirement.
ai_technique: Time Series Forecasting with education staffing model
how_it_works: Ingests three data streams: enrollment forecasts by campus and program (from the enrollment system), historical attrition rates by role, tenure band, and location, and planned operational events (campus openings, program launches, known retirements from HRIS). Combines these into a staffing demand model that outputs a 12-month rolling hiring plan: 'August: 12 primary teachers needed (7 attrition replacement + 5 growth); October: 4 STEM teachers; January: 2 principals.' Refreshes monthly. Surfaces the sourcing lead-time required for each role type so HR knows when to activate recruitment.
buyer_persona: Head of HR or Talent Planning Manager at multi-site education organization
scoring_rationale: Strong problem: reactive hiring in education is expensive and directly affects teaching quality. Forecast accuracy depends on having reliable enrollment projections and structured attrition data, which may be partially available. Best positioned as a module that consumes outputs from the Teacher Attrition Risk Forecast and Cohort Retention Predictor rather than a standalone product.

name: Teacher Interview Scoring Assistant
one_liner: Converts unstructured interview notes into a structured competency scorecard against teaching rubrics — so hiring committees compare candidates on evidence, not memory.
problem: Principals and HR panels at CSPD and School for Life conduct 10–20 interviews per teaching role but assess candidates from handwritten notes or subjective memory. Without a shared rubric, committee decisions are inconsistent: strong candidates are passed over because they interviewed with a tougher panel; weak candidates advance because their interviewer was lenient. Post-hire mismatches are common and expensive.
ai_technique: Summarization & Condensation with competency rubric alignment
how_it_works: Accepts interview notes in any format — typed summaries emailed by interviewers, transcript exports from recorded interviews, or structured form submissions. Runs each set of notes through a domain-specific extraction model that scores the candidate on five teaching competencies: pedagogical approach, subject content knowledge, classroom management philosophy, pastoral and wellbeing awareness, and cultural fit signals. Generates a one-page structured scorecard per candidate with a 1–5 rating per dimension, supporting quotes from the notes, and a summary of red flags. For panels interviewing multiple candidates for the same role, produces a side-by-side comparison table.
buyer_persona: Recruiting Manager or Principal involved in teacher hiring panel
scoring_rationale: Easy to build and fast to deploy — no external API dependencies, input is interview notes that already exist. Low defensibility: the core is prompt engineering over interview text, which any education HR tool could replicate quickly. Pain is real but not severe enough to be a primary purchase driver; most valuable as a component of a broader hiring workflow product.

name: Teacher Onboarding Curriculum Builder
one_liner: Generates a personalized onboarding plan for each new teacher — varying the depth, resources, and milestone schedule based on their experience level, subject area, and the campus they are joining.
problem: New teachers at Rising Academies and PHINMA receive the same generic onboarding regardless of whether they are a 10-year experienced hire or a graduate joining their first classroom. The experienced hire wastes days on basic orientation; the graduate gets no structured mentoring in the critical first 8 weeks when early attrition risk is highest. Inconsistency across 80+ campuses means onboarding quality depends on which administrator happens to be available.
ai_technique: Data Transformation & Structuring with personalization logic
how_it_works: Ingests the incoming teacher's profile (years of experience, subject, prior school type, role level) and the campus context (curriculum stage, accreditation status, enrollment trend, available mentor teachers). Selects from a library of onboarding modules — curriculum guides, NESA standard mapping, parent communication templates, pedagogical frameworks — and assembles a sequenced plan with week-by-week milestones. Graduate teachers receive an 8-week structured program with peer mentoring assigned by week 1; experienced hires get a 2-week targeted integration. Progress is tracked and the system flags a new hire as at-risk if key milestones slip past their target date.
buyer_persona: Onboarding Coordinator or Head of New Teacher Support
scoring_rationale: Low buyer clarity — 'Onboarding Coordinator' rarely has budget authority, and this problem is easy to dismiss as 'just use a checklist.' Build is feasible but the output is a document, not a system, making it hard to price as SaaS. Better positioned as a onboarding wizard feature inside a broader Staffroom HR product than a standalone offering.

name: Inquiry Intent Classifier
one_liner: Classifies incoming student and parent inquiries by enrollment intent and risk across SMS, WhatsApp, and email — giving admissions staff a prioritized call list instead of a raw message queue.
problem: Admissions teams at Rising Academies and iPeople manually triage hundreds of weekly inquiries arriving across SMS, WhatsApp, and email. In markets where conversion rates are 5–15%, slow or misdirected responses cost enrollments. High-intent families who need one follow-up call get the same response time as passive browsers. Early signals of dropout risk — 'might not be able to afford fees next term' — go undetected in the message pile.
how_it_works: Connects to WhatsApp Business API, email inbox, and web form submissions. Classifies each message: high-intent enrollment, scholarship inquiry, fee-payment concern, program comparison, or dropout-risk flag. Routes high-intent inquiries to an admissions rep for same-day follow-up. Sends dropout-risk signals to a student success officer with the underlying reason extracted from the message text. Generates a weekly funnel report showing conversion by intent category and response-time impact on conversion rate.
buyer_persona: Director of Admissions or Enrollment Manager
scoring_rationale: This idea is functionally identical to 'Enrollment Intent Classifier' (idea 5 in this batch) and also duplicates ideas 17, 39, and 72 in the full list. The concept is strong — high ROI, fast deployment, clear buyer — but the duplication across the 81-idea list suggests these should be consolidated into a single well-specified product rather than treated as distinct opportunities.
